coredata.dc:title: Formation of vesicles between negatively charged carbon quantum dots and cationic surfactant cetylpyridinium chloride (CPC) due to oxidative photo induced electron transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Rooj, Bipin
Pramanik, Anup
Mandal, Ujjwal
coredata.dc:description: The tuning of photo physical properties of highly emissive carbon quantum dots which was synthesized from citric acid in DMF medium through solvothermal method is investigated inside micelles formed by different types of surfactants like cationic surfactant cetylpyridinium chloride (CPC), cetrimonium bromide (CTAB), anionic surfactant sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and neutral surfactant polyethylene glycol tert-octyl phenyl ether (TX100), polyoxoethylene lauryl ether (BRIJ-35). Interestingly, only CPC interacts with greater extent and formed co-assembly with negatively-charged carbon quantum dots (CQDs) whereas other surfactants like CTAB, SDS, TX100 and BRIJ-35 do not alter the fluorescence intensity extensively. This results in the formation of a non fluorescent vesicle. No phase separation was observed over the large concentration barrier of the CPC surfactant. However, no such vesicle formation takes place for another cationic surfactant CTAB excluding the role of only electrostatic interaction. The synthesized vesicle is characterized by various techniques including HRTEM, DLS. For the aqueous solution of CQDs having concentration of 0.5 mg/ml, addition of CPC leads to formation of different sized vesicles of size 40 nm to 100 nm. The driving force behind this formation of non-fluorescent vesicles is oxidative photo induced electron transfer which is revealed by the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) –lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) interaction between the donor (CQDs) and the acceptor (CPC). We trust such type vesicle can be a suitable alternative for drug carriers, biomarkers and other various applications also.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
CPC
Micelle
Fluorescence
Vesicle
Photo induced electron transfer

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors would like to thank Department of Chemistry, The University of Burdwan for providing infrastructural and instrumental facility. U. M. acknowledges UGC, New Delhi for giving UGC-BSR start up grant [Ref. No. F.30-383/2017(BSR)] and SERB for research funding [File no EEQ /2018/ 000964].

Authors would like to thank Department of Chemistry, The University of Burdwan for providing infrastructural and instrumental facility. U. M. acknowledges UGC, New Delhi for giving UGC-BSR start up grant [Ref. No. F.30-383/2017(BSR)] and SERB for research funding [File no EEQ /2018/ 000964].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the synthesized CQDs.
Size distribution histogram for CQDs only.
Excitation wavelength dependent emission of CQDs.
Fluorescence Property of CQDs in presence of various surfactants with concentration variation from pre CMC to post CMC.
Plot of I/I0 vs. CPC concentration.
TEM image of the vesicles and its size distribution histogram.
Schematic representation of the synthesis and self assembly process of CQDs and surfactant.
Relative positions of HOMO and LUMO energy states of different surfactants and their energy values.
Quantum yields of Coumarin 153(C153) in DMF Quantum yields of CQDs in DMF.
HOMO, LUMO energy value of different surfactants and their energy gap.

head.ce:title: Formation of vesicles between negatively charged carbon quantum dots and cationic surfactant cetylpyridinium chloride (CPC) due to oxidative photo induced electron transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The University of Burdwan, Golapbag, Burdwan 713104, West Bengal, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The University of Burdwan, Golapbag, Burdwan 713104, West Bengal, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sidho-Kanho-Birsha University, Purulia 723104, West Bengal, India,
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sidho-Kanho-Birsha University, Purulia 723104, West Bengal, India,

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The tuning of photo physical properties of highly emissive carbon quantum dots which was synthesized from citric acid in DMF medium through solvothermal method is investigated inside micelles formed by different types of surfactants like cationic surfactant cetylpyridinium chloride (CPC), cetrimonium bromide (CTAB), anionic surfactant sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and neutral surfactant polyethylene glycol tert-octyl phenyl ether (TX100), polyoxoethylene lauryl ether (BRIJ-35). Interestingly, only CPC interacts with greater extent and formed co-assembly with negatively-charged carbon quantum dots (CQDs) whereas other surfactants like CTAB, SDS, TX100 and BRIJ-35 do not alter the fluorescence intensity extensively. This results in the formation of a non fluorescent vesicle. No phase separation was observed over the large concentration barrier of the CPC surfactant. However, no such vesicle formation takes place for another cationic surfactant CTAB excluding the role of only electrostatic interaction. The synthesized vesicle is characterized by various techniques including HRTEM, DLS. For the aqueous solution of CQDs having concentration of 0.5 mg/ml, addition of CPC leads to formation of different sized vesicles of size 40 nm to 100 nm. The driving force behind this formation of non-fluorescent vesicles is oxidative photo induced electron transfer which is revealed by the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) –lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) interaction between the donor (CQDs) and the acceptor (CPC). We trust such type vesicle can be a suitable alternative for drug carriers, biomarkers and other various applications also.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vesicles are a structure which consist a hollow aqueous core with cell membrane mimicking hydrophobic lipid bilayer [1–3]. Vesicles have drawn lots of attention of research owing to its several applications in drug delivery, cytomimetic and also can acts as templates for nano materials synthesis [4–8]. In the past work there are numberless works where different amphiphilic molecules and their mixtures have been used for vesicle formation in aqueous medium [9,10]. These vesicles formation are also performed by taking phospholipids, various types of surfactants, hyper branched polymers, block copolymers, fatty acids, dendrimers, polypeptides etc [11–17]. There lots of work where it is exhibited that various functionalized nanoparticles (NPs) can be used in this purpose also [18,19]. These days, efforts have been made to increase the functional efficiency of these systems by modifying vesicles and introducing various functional groups. It has been revealed that vesicles have a low capacity to carry drugs or biomolecules. Global research has been done on the use of protein, enzyme, DNA, and RNA attachment into vesicles to create better materials. Carbon dots, which have fascinating mechanical, chemical, magnetic, optical, and electrical properties efforts have been made to, increase the functional efficiency of these systems by modifying vesicles and introducing various functional groups. Thus following the above ways we can easily introduce different uniqueness in the so formed vesicles. In the past few decades, a comprehensive study have been performed to get better inside about the structural and functional information about the all types of vesicles as they are very important to understand in the view point of application in different field of science [20–22].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) dots which are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles having size less than 10 nm are superior due to their high extent solubility in water, highly resistant to chemical activity, low toxicity and good biocompatibility which makes them highly demanding on the field of bio imaging bio sensing [23–28], sensing of inorganic analytes [29,30], temperature sensing [31], drug delivery [32,33], biomedicine [34,35], UV light absorbents [36] etc. The photo physical properties, high extent of chemical stability and their non-toxicity enhance their application in bio-logical field [37–39]. The solubility of different pharmaceutical drugs is an important issue for their cell activity [40,41]. CQDs attached with different drugs through π–π stacking enhances their solubility, biological stability as well as their extent of cell penetration capability [42–44] and can extend the tumor retention period, and reduce the drugs’ side effects [45–47], so becomes a suitable carrier of drugs for biological cell [48–51]. The less solubility and lack of cell penetration makes a lot of pharmaceutical drugs limited for their bio-medical application. Recently, nano drug delivery systems, becomes an important aspect for therapeutic field [52–57] which is looked forward to achieve enhanced therapeutic efficiency and simultaneously lowering the side effects, have gained much research interest in the past few years. Some of the recent work shows that CQDs can enter inside the nucleus of a carcinoma cell and thus it’s a giant step towards the success of eradication of cancer [46,58,59]. According to various reports many drugs gets hydrolyzed in aqua medium [60,61]. Thus application of various drugs becomes more challenging. Thus an alternative way for the encapsulation of various drugs attached with carbon quantum dots in various self-assembled organized systems like micelles vesicles proteins and polymers is of paramount important of interest [62–65]. This can overcome all sorts of problems and makes it highly effective, highly soluble and more cells friendly.

Different photo physical phenomena in such confined medium lead an important aspect in different biological process [66]. The attachment of drugs with carbon quantum dots in different parts of synthesized vesicles helps us to get desirable properties of the system. This type of vesicles can impose desirable allocation to the guest attached to carbon quantum dots to provide better penetration, protection to aqua medium [67–71]. Vesicles are analogous in structure to the vesicles formed by phospholipids (liposome)[72]. In the past decade, vesicles are extensively applied as an alternative medium to delivery drugs because of their biocompatibility and low toxicity [73,74]. On other side vesicles have lots of benefits over liposome due to their high stability, easy to synthesize and low coast of surfactants which makes it more attractive to various fields of science. Vesicles are suitable agent to soluble both the hydrophobic and hydrophilic drugs and also its stability in aqua medium [75–77].

In this work, we have synthesized a water-soluble vesicle by the combination of different surfactants and newly synthesized negatively charged carbon quantum dots but vesicles only formed between the positively charged CPC surfactant and the CQDs. It has been also observed that the charge of the surfactant that is electrostatic force of attraction does not play any important role here as there observed no vesicle formation takes place between positively charged surfactant CTAB and negatively charged CQDs. The only driving force involved in this process is the oxidative photo induced electron transfer.

Citric acid, dimethyl formamide, CPC, CTAB, SDS, TX-100, Brij-35. Laser grade coumarin 153 was purchased from Exciton. Deionised water is used in all our experiments (produced by a water purification system Q-Grad1, Millipore). The weighing balance (Sartorius BSA224S-CW) was used to weigh out the entire chemical reagent used. Stock solutions of the surfactant were prepared by using Digital Ultrasonic Cleaner CD 4820 instrument. Surfactant substrate mixture solution and other mixture solutions containing the surfactant were centrifuged to get a complete mixing through Centrifuge-Z206A (Hermle Labortechnik GmbH).The absorbance of the synthesized CQDs is measured in a 1 cm quartz cell with a SHIMADZU-3600 UV–VIS NIR spectrophotometer fitted with an SHIMADZU thermostatically controlled cell block. Infrared (IR; in KBr; Perkin-Elmer FTIR model RX1 spectrophotometer) spectra were recorded. The TEM images are taken by the Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100) instrument using dried ultrathin carbon-coated copper grid coated with aqueous solution of CQDs and vesicles that are formed with CPC surfactant operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The fluorescence measurement experiment during CMC measurement of CPC was carried out by using Perkin-Elmer (LS 55) spectrofluorimeter and the slit width of excitation and emission were set both as 2.5 nm with scanning rate of 300 nm/min.

CQDs were synthesized using a green and cost-effective method. Here 1.0 g Citric acid and 60 mL DMF was taken in Teflon lined autoclave. The autoclave was placed in the dry chamber keeping the temperature at 250 °C for one and half hour. After taking the autoclave out of the hot oven it was cooled to room temperature immediately by immersing the tightly sealed autoclave into a water bath. The color of the resulting solution was found to be deep yellow. The solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min. Afterwards the solution was filtered using a Whatmann 42 filter paper. No residual was found. The yellow color solution was then transferred into a volumetric flask and kept in room temperature (Scheme 1 ). The resulting CQDs were purified by dialysis for 24 h. The resulting dark yellowish solution is rotavapoured at 80 °C to remove solvent and the obtained dark black substances are kept in vacuum desiccators. The highly water-soluble compounds are dissolved in water at a concentration of 0.5 mg per mL of water, refrigerated, and employed in subsequent studies.

Using a UV–VIS 2450 (Shimadzu, Japan) spectrophotometer we recorded the absorption spectra of CQDs in DMF solvent. For both absorption and emission study quartz cuvettes of path length 1 cm are used as a sample holder. No distinct absorption maximum was present for the CQD prepared in DMF solvent. Hitachi F-7000 spectrofluorimeter is used for recording the steady-state emission and excitation spectra. To characterize the size and shape of the CQDs we use Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100). TEM specimen, prepared in a given volume of CQDs dispersion is dropped on a carbon coated copper grid, followed by drying in a vacuum oven for overnight. From TEM image the size of the quantum dots were calculated to be ∼ 4 nm.

TCSPC was used for recording the decay of CQDs. The sample was excited at 375 nm and data was collected at a magic angle polarization. The time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements (lifetime and fluorescence anisotropy measurements) were carried out by the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) method on a FluoroCube-01-NL spectrometer using a laser diode (model: DD-450L-8666) as the light source for excitation of the samples at λex = 450 nm. The signals were collected at the magic angle polarization of 54.7° in order to eliminate the contribution from fluorescence depolarization. We measured the life time of the CQDs both with and without surfactant.

If a system contains various sizes of CQDs, then the smaller quantum dots are preferentially excited at shorter wavelength that means higher energy region and gives emission spectra towards blue region and the bigger size quantum dots get excited at higher wavelength that means lower energy region and they emit light at relatively lower energy region, red edge. But in our case, we did not observe any excitation wavelength dependent emission property of the as prepared CQDs. The emission maximum was found from excitation emission spectra at 385 nm. When we excited the CQDs at 385 nm, the emission maxima was found at 432 nm. Excitation at other wavelength like 375 nm or 395 nm etc, did not give any fluorescence, at all. So from above discussion it can be stated that there is no poly dispersity or local heterogeneity in the CQDs system.

The Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was measured using coumarin 153 (C153) as a reference. C153 has a reported QY of 54.6 % in ethanol[79]. We first measured the QY of C153 in DMF taking the quantum yield of C153 in ethanol as standard. Absorption and emission of C153 in DMF and in ethanol at low concentration was measured. Wavelength integrated emission intensity of C153 in DMF and ethanol was calculated. Using the following equation QY of C153 in DMF was calculated [Table 1 ].
QY =
QY
s
I
I s
A s
A
η
2
η
s
2

Where QY is the quantum yield of the sample, QYs is the quantum yield of the reference. I and A are the corresponding absorption and emission of the sample. Is and As are the absorption and emission of the reference. η is the refractive index of the medium. QY of CQDs was measured following the same procedure adopted for measuring the QY of C153 in DMF. The QY of CQDs was found to be ∼ 39%.

Emission spectra of CQDs are recorded in presence of various surfactants (CPC, CTAB, SDS, TX-100, BRIJ-35). At very low surfactant concentration emission of CQDs was unaffected. As we increase the surfactant concentration the emission of CQDs was found to be quenched in case of CPC only [Fig. 6]. The other surfactant (CTAB, SDS, TX-100, and BRIJ-35) doesn’t affect the CQDs emission property at their very high concentration also. We increase the concentration of CPC stepwise and the emission intensity was found to decrease steadily. We use the Stern-Volmer plot to calculate the LOD of CPC. The effect of surfactant concentration on the emission intensity of CQDs is shown in Fig. 6. The Stern-Volmer plot follows a linear relation up to a CPC concentration of 4000 μM. The concentration of CPC was varied from 80 μM to 4000 μM. and above 4000 μM Stern-Volmer plot becomes non linear. The correlation between CPC and fluorescence intensity obtained from the stern-Volmer plot [81] was used to calculate the LOD (3σ) of CPC is found to be ∼ 4 µM.

Vesicles are of foremost important in biomedicine and for material science also [82,83]. It will be great if we found an alternative way to design a multifunctional material, vesicles by the appropriate combination of surfactant and CQDs with greater inside information of the driving force that helps them to gather and converts into vesicles and that will also augment of theory of vesicle formation. In this work, we are first time able to form a hybrid vesicle between newly synthesized CQDs and oppositely charged surfactant by simple addition method without any phase separation, also reveals the driving force behind that vesicle formation. We observed that only electrostatic force is not responsible for this vesicle formation process but other things are involved.

To find the reason behind the formation the zeta potential of the aqueous CQDs only and the mixture of the surfactants are also studied. The ξ of 0.5 mg L−1 CQDs in water (pH 7) is found to be −14.2 ± 0.2 mV which indicates the CQDs are negatively charged [Fig. 8 ]. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs is approximately 100% diminished close to the concentration of the CPC is 1.5 mM. This concentration can also be regarded as the critical when the attachment of the molecules of the CPC surfactant over the surface of the CQDs is getting saturated.

From the observation we can easily conclude that the vesicle formation takes place due to negatively charged CQDs and positively charged CPC surfactant but this thought may not be the only responsible factor as it is partially nullified as there is no evidence of formation of vesicles with CTAB surfactant. To find out the reason the band gap values for the synthesized CQDs have been investigated from the optical absorption phenomenon which showed that the fundamental absorption spectra obey Tauc’s formula[84] for the allowed direct transition.
(αhυ)n = K(hυ – Eɡ)
Where hυ is the energy of photon, α is the absorption coefficient, K is a constant, Eɡ is the band gap energy and the n is 2 for direct allowed transition and ½ for indirect allowed transition.

The absorption coefficient (α) and the incident photon energy (hν) are related with the Tauc’s formula. The variation of (αhν)2 vs. hν for the CQDs shows that the extrapolation of the linear portion to the hν axis gives the energy gap of the direct band gap. We can see that the value of the direct band gap for the so synthesized CQDs is 3.65 eV [Fig. 9 ].

In summary we are succeed to synthesize carbon quantum dots having quantum yield 39% from citric acid using DMF as solvent. We have systematically investigated the interaction of various surfactants like CPC, CTAB, TX-100, Brij-35 and SDS with the so synthesized negatively charged CQDs. Among all of them only CPC interacts with the CQDs leading formation of non fluorescent CQDs-CPC vesicles with narrow size distribution without any phase separation. We are also able to reveals the fact that this vesicles formation takes place due to oxidative PET process not due to electrostatic force of interaction as a whole thing between CQDs and CPC in spite of another cationic surfactant CTAB. This study also contributes a lot to the theory of vesicles formation. In the later work we can employ these vesicles as an alternative drug carrier.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank Department of Chemistry, The University of Burdwan for providing infrastructural and instrumental facility. U. M. acknowledges UGC, New Delhi for giving UGC-BSR start up grant [Ref. No. F.30-383/2017(BSR)] and SERB for research funding [File no EEQ /2018/ 000964].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2023.123632.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
